subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! In this unit you will master two of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics: ratio and proportion. A ratio is a way of comparing quantities – how much of one thing there is for every amount of another. Proportion is what happens when you keep that relationship the same while the amounts grow or shrink. Together they let you scale a recipe up for a big Eid gathering, read the distance between Hargeisa and Berbera off a map, mix concrete in the correct strength, or share the profit from a market stall fairly between two partners.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Write and interpret a ratio using colon notation such as 3:5, and read it aloud correctly.
Explain why the order of the numbers in a ratio matters.
Tell apart part-to-part and part-to-whole ratios.
Write ratios in the same units before comparing, and simplify by dividing to the same unit.
Simplify a ratio to its lowest terms using the highest common factor (HCF), including ratios with decimals or fractions.
Find equivalent ratios and use a ratio table to solve missing-value problems.
Share a quantity in a given ratio using the parts (unitary) method.
Recognise and solve direct proportion problems using the unitary method and scaling.
Use scale and scale factors to work between map, model or drawing sizes and real sizes.

concepts.title: Understanding Ratios
concepts.explanation: A ratio is a way of comparing two or more quantities by showing how much of one there is for every amount of another. We usually write a ratio using the colon symbol. If Farah’s flock has 3 brown goats and 5 white goats, we write the ratio of brown to white as 3:5 and read it as “three to five”. It tells us that for every 3 brown goats there are 5 white goats. Each number in a ratio is called a term. In the ratio 3:5, the first term (3) is the number of brown goats and the second term (5) is the number of white…
concepts.example: The order of the terms is not decoration – it carries meaning. The ratio 3:5 is not the same as 5:3. “Brown to white is 3:5” says there are fewer brown goats; “white to brown is 5:3” says the same flock the other way…

concepts.title: Simplifying Ratios
concepts.explanation: To simplify a ratio means to write it using the smallest possible whole numbers while keeping exactly the same relationship. It works just like simplifying a fraction: you divide every term by the highest common factor (HCF) – the largest number that divides into all the terms exactly. Let us simplify 12:18 step by step. First list the factors: the factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12, and the factors of 18 are 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18. The common factors are 1, 2, 3 and 6, so the HCF is 6. Now divide both terms by 6: 12…
concepts.example: Here is the reliable method to follow every time:

concepts.title: Equivalent Ratios and Ratio Tables
concepts.explanation: Equivalent ratios describe the same relationship using different numbers, just as equivalent fractions describe the same portion of a whole. The ratios 2:3, 4:6, 6:9 and 8:12 are all equivalent because they all simplify to 2:3. To build an equivalent ratio, you multiply or divide every term by the same non-zero number. Starting from 3:5, multiply both terms by 2 to get 6:10, by 3 to get 9:15, and by 4 to get 12:20. Every one of these is equivalent to 3:5. Going the other way, dividing both terms of 12:20 by 4…
concepts.example: Testing Whether Two Ratios Are Equivalent

concepts.title: Sharing a Quantity in a Given Ratio
concepts.explanation: One of the most useful things you can do with a ratio is share an amount fairly. Suppose Aisha and Ibrahim run a market stall together and share the day’s profit of 240 shillings in the ratio 3:5, because Ibrahim worked more hours. How much does each person get? The parts method makes this simple and safe. Here is the method, which always works:
concepts.example: Add the terms of the ratio to find the total number of parts: 3 + 5 = 8 parts.

concepts.title: Direct Proportion and the Unitary Method
concepts.explanation: A proportion is simply a statement that two ratios are equal, such as 2:3 = 6:9. Two quantities are in direct proportion when they grow together at the same rate: as one doubles, so does the other; as one halves, so does the other. The cost of mangoes is in direct proportion to how many you buy (at a fixed price each). The distance a bus travels is in direct proportion to the time it drives (at a steady speed). In every case the ratio between the two quantities stays constant. The most powerful tool for direct…
concepts.example: Worked Idea: Using the Unitary Method

concepts.title: Scale, Maps and Scale Factors
concepts.explanation: Scale is a special use of ratio that compares the size of a map, drawing or model with the real object it represents. A scale lets us show something enormous, like the road between two cities, on a small sheet of paper, or something tiny in a larger, clearer drawing. A map scale of 1:50 000 means that 1 unit on the map stands for 50 000 of the same units in real life. A scale factor tells you how many times bigger or smaller one figure is than another. If a model car is built at a scale of 1:50, then every real…
concepts.example: Maps turn scale into a practical skill. On a 1:50 000 map, 1 cm on the paper represents 50 000 cm in reality. Since 50 000 cm = 500 m = 0.5 km, every centimetre on this map is half a kilometre on the ground. To find a…

methods.title: How to simplify a ratio
methods.example: In a class of 30 students, 12 are boys and 18 are girls. Write the ratio of boys to girls in its simplest form.
steps: Make sure all terms are in the same unit.
Find the HCF of all the terms.
Divide every term by the HCF.
Check that no number still divides into all the terms.

methods.title: How to share an amount in a ratio
methods.example: Write the ratio of 2 hours to 90 minutes in its simplest form.
steps: Add the terms to find the total number of parts.
Divide the amount by the total parts to find one part.
Multiply each term by the value of one part.
Check by adding all the shares back to the original amount.

methods.title: How to solve direct proportion (unitary method)
steps: Find the value of one unit by dividing.
Multiply by the number of units you want.
Check the answer makes sense in the real situation.

methods.title: Find a missing value
methods.example: The ratio of flour to sugar is 3:2. Farah uses 9 cups of flour. How much sugar does she need?
steps: Solution: Set up equivalent ratios 3:2 = 9:?.
Since 3 was multiplied by 3 to give 9, multiply the sugar term by 3 as well: 2 × 3 = 6 cups of sugar.
Check the find a missing value result carefully.

methods.title: Share in a ratio
methods.example: Aisha and Ibrahim share 240 shillings in the ratio 3:5. How much does each get?
steps: Solution: Total parts = 3 + 5 = 8.
One part = 240 ÷ 8 = 30 shillings.
Aisha gets 3 × 30 = 90; Ibrahim gets 5 × 30 = 150.

methods.title: The unitary method
methods.example: If 5 pencils cost 15 shillings, how much do 8 pencils cost?
steps: Solution: Cost of one pencil = 15 ÷ 5 = 3 shillings.
Cost of 8 pencils = 8 × 3 = 24 shillings.
Check the the unitary method result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Write and simplify a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: In a class of 30 students, 12 are boys and 18 are girls. Write the ratio of boys to girls in its simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The ratio of boys to girls is 12:18. The HCF of 12 and 18 is 6, so divide both terms by 6: (12÷6):(18÷6) = 2:3. For every 2 boys there are 3 girls.

workedExamples.title: Ratio with different units
workedExamples.prompt: Write the ratio of 2 hours to 90 minutes in its simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First make the units match. 2 hours = 120 minutes, so the ratio is 120:90. The HCF of 120 and 90 is 30, so (120÷30):(90÷30) = 4:3.

workedExamples.title: Clear the decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply both terms by 10 to clear the decimals: 15:25. The HCF of 15 and 25 is 5, so (15÷5):(25÷5) = 3:5.

workedExamples.title: Find a missing value
workedExamples.prompt: The ratio of flour to sugar is 3:2. Farah uses 9 cups of flour. How much sugar does she need?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Set up equivalent ratios 3:2 = 9:?. Since 3 was multiplied by 3 to give 9, multiply the sugar term by 3 as well: 2 × 3 = 6 cups of sugar.

workedExamples.title: Share in a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: Aisha and Ibrahim share 240 shillings in the ratio 3:5. How much does each get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total parts = 3 + 5 = 8. One part = 240 ÷ 8 = 30 shillings. Aisha gets 3 × 30 = 90; Ibrahim gets 5 × 30 = 150. Check: 90 + 150 = 240. Correct.

workedExamples.title: The unitary method
workedExamples.prompt: If 5 pencils cost 15 shillings, how much do 8 pencils cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cost of one pencil = 15 ÷ 5 = 3 shillings. Cost of 8 pencils = 8 × 3 = 24 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Sum and ratio together
workedExamples.prompt: Two numbers are in the ratio 7:4, and their sum is 66. Find the two numbers.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total parts = 7 + 4 = 11. One part = 66 ÷ 11 = 6. The numbers are 7 × 6 = 42 and 4 × 6 = 24. Check: 42 + 24 = 66. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Map scale
workedExamples.prompt: Two towns are 3 cm apart on a 1:50 000 map. What is the real distance in kilometres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Real distance = 3 × 50 000 = 150 000 cm. Convert: 150 000 cm = 1 500 m = 1.5 km.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: If 6 sambusas cost 30 shillings, how much do 10 sambusas cost?

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A recipe for 4 people uses 2 cups of rice. How much rice is needed for 10 people?
workedExamples.solution: Total parts 7; one part 50; shares 150 and 200.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: The ratio of red to blue beads is 3:7. There are 21 red beads. How many blue beads and how many beads in total?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Two numbers are in the ratio 5:3 and their difference is 16. Find the two numbers.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Difference is 5 − 3 = 2 parts = 16, so one part = 8; numbers are 40 and 24.

activities.title: The Perfect Juice Mix
activities.materials: cups, water, and a squash, juice or milk you like.
steps: Choose a mixing ratio, for example 1 part juice to 4 parts water (1:4).
Measure out 1 cup of juice and 4 cups of water and stir.
Now double the drink for more guests: use 2 cups of juice and 8 cups of water. Notice the ratio stays 1:4.
Try a stronger mix, such as 1:3, and taste the difference. Which ratio do your family prefer?
This shows how equivalent ratios keep the same taste even when you make more.

activities.title: Scale Up the Recipe (Family Cooking)
activities.materials: a simple recipe (bariis, anjero or a cake) and a family member to cook with.
steps: Find a recipe and note how many people it serves.
Count how many people you actually want to feed and work out the scaling factor.
Multiply every ingredient by the scaling factor and write the new amounts.
Cook it together and check that the food still tastes right – proof that proportion works!

activities.title: Fair Shares Game
activities.materials: a pile of counters, dried beans, stones or dates.
steps: Count out a total, for example 40 beans.
Pick a sharing ratio such as 3:5 and add the parts: 3 + 5 = 8.
Find one part (40 ÷ 8 = 5) and deal out the two shares (15 and 25).
Check the shares add back to the total, then try new totals and ratios.
This is exactly how market partners split their profit fairly.

activities.title: Map Distance Investigation
activities.materials: a printed or drawn map with a scale, and a ruler.
steps: Find the scale printed on the map, for example 1:50 000.
Measure the straight distance between two places in centimetres.
Multiply by the scale to find the real distance, then convert to kilometres.
Check three different pairs of places and record your results in a small table.

activities.title: Build a Scale Model
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and something to measure such as a table or a room.
steps: Measure the real length and width of a table or room in centimetres.
Choose a scale factor, for example 1:20, so every real length is divided by 20.
Draw the scaled rectangle on paper using your calculated measurements.
Add scaled furniture and label each length. How realistic does your plan look?

activities.title: Ratio Hunt (Investigation)
activities.materials: a notebook and your everyday surroundings.
steps: Spend one day spotting ratios around you: goats to sheep, boys to girls, price of two items.
Write down at least five real ratios you find.
Simplify each ratio to its lowest terms.
Share your list with your teacher and explain what each ratio compares.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand ratio and proportion best? Where did you see a constant ratio staying the same as amounts grew? What was the trickiest part, and what would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Ratio Basics A ratio compares quantities in the same unit, written with a colon, e.g. 3:5. Order matters: 3:5 is not the same as 5:3. Convert to the same unit before writing or simplifying a ratio.

rules.title: Simplifying Rule
rules.text: Divide every term by the highest common factor (HCF). Clear decimals by multiplying by 10, 100, and so on, then simplify. Clear fractions by multiplying by the LCM of the denominators, then simplify.

rules.title: Sharing Rule
rules.text: Add the terms to get the total number of parts. Divide the amount by the total parts to find one part. Multiply each term by one part; check the shares add to the total.

rules.title: Proportion and Scale Rule
rules.text: Unitary method: find the value of one unit, then multiply up. Real size = drawing or map size multiplied by the scale. Drawing or map size = real size divided by the scale.

terms: A comparison of quantities in the same unit
One of the numbers in a ratio
Divide all terms by the HCF
Different ratios showing the same relationship
A statement that two ratios are equal
Both quantities grow at the same rate
Find the value of one unit, then scale up
How many times larger or smaller a figure is
commonMistakes: Writing 5:3 for boys-to-girls when it should be 3:5
Match the order to the words in the question
Convert to the same unit first: 120:90 = 4:3
Dividing only one term when simplifying
Divide every term by the same HCF
Dividing by a single term when sharing
Add the terms first, then divide by the total parts

realProblems.prompt: At Eastleigh market, Sumaya mixes a drink using 2 parts syrup to 5 parts water. She wants 21 cups of drink in total. How much syrup and how much water does she use?
realProblems.answer: 1) Total parts 7; one part = 3 cups; syrup 6 cups, water 15 cups.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Total parts 7; one part = 3 cups; syrup 6 cups, water 15 cups.

selfAssessment: I can write and interpret a ratio using colon notation such as 3:5, and read it aloud correctly.
I can explain why the order of the numbers in a ratio matters.
I can tell apart part-to-part and part-to-whole ratios.
I can write ratios in the same units before comparing, and simplify by dividing to the same unit.
I can simplify a ratio to its lowest terms using the highest common factor (HCF), including ratios with decimals or fractions.
I can find equivalent ratios and use a ratio table to solve missing-value problems.
I can share a quantity in a given ratio using the parts (unitary) method.
I can recognise and solve direct proportion problems using the unitary method and scaling.
